questions.question: What is the speculative-decoding pitch, in numbers?
options: Halving the model's parameter count
A 3B draft proposes 5 tokens (~3ms each), then the 70B verifies all 5 in ONE forward pass (~30ms): 45ms total vs 150ms straight-line — trading a little GPU memory for 2-4x lower decode latency
Skipping every other output token
Compressing the KV cache by 4x
questions.explanation: It attacks the autoregressive tax directly: verification is parallel even though generation is serial, so let a cheap model do the serial part.

questions.question: What guarantee makes speculative sampling safe to deploy?
options: Outputs are at most 5% worse
The output sequence is IDENTICALLY DISTRIBUTED to what the big model would have produced alone (Leviathan 2023, Chen 2023) — no quality trade, just speed
It only works with greedy decoding
The draft model is fine-tuned to agree
questions.explanation: Accept each draft token with probability min(1, q/p); on rejection, sample from the normalized residual (q−p)₊. The residual trick is the mathematical insight preserving exactness.

questions.question: What happens on rejection, and what's the bonus on full acceptance?
options: The whole sequence restarts

On first rejection at position j, sample the replacement from the normalized residual (q_j − p_j)₊ and discard later drafts; if all N drafts are accepted, sample one extra token free from q_{N+1} — the verifier pass already computed it
Rejected tokens are retried with the draft
Rejections fall back to greedy decoding
questions.explanation: Left-to-right accept/reject plus the residual correction is the whole algorithm — one big-model call yields between 1 and N+1 tokens.

questions.question: What determines the acceptance rate α?
options: Only the size of the verifier

Draft-verifier similarity (same family/training data boosts it), decoding strategy (greedy-on-greedy accepts most; temperature sampling drops it), and task type — code and structured output accept more than free-form creative writing
The GPU memory bandwidth
The vocabulary size alone
questions.explanation: Rule of thumb: at α = 0.75 with N = 5, you make ~3x fewer big-model calls, netting ~2.5x wall-clock after paying the cheap draft cost.

questions.question: How do the four 2026 draft-verifier families differ?
options: They are all the same algorithm renamed

Vanilla uses a separate small draft model; Medusa adds extra decoding heads on the verifier itself (no draft model); EAGLE reuses the verifier's hidden states for a lightweight draft with higher acceptance (3-4x typical); Lookahead self-speculates via Jacobi iteration with no draft at all
They differ only in batch size
Only vanilla is used in production
questions.explanation: Every production stack — vLLM, TensorRT-LLM, SGLang, llama.cpp — ships at least vanilla + EAGLE-2 by default in 2026.
